ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mee Tai Bak or silver needle noodles; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for Mee Tai Bak silver needle noodles in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Mee Tai Bak or silver needle noodle dish.

main_ingredients: mee tai bak (silver needle noodles / rice flour noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of wok fried mee tai bak weighs approximately 350 g, comprising noodles, egg, bean sprouts, and seasoning.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Kway Teow / Stir-fried rice noodles — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Mee Tai Bak (silver needle noodles) is a short, stubby rice-flour noodle with a nutritional profile closely analogous to other stir-fried rice noodle dishes such as fried kway teow and fried bee hoon in the HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo stir-fried rice noodle entries (fried kway teow / fried bee hoon) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy rice noodle cooked data (FDC ID 168930) to produce a defensible per-100g estimate for wok-fried mee tai bak. The dish is predominantly rice-flour noodles with egg, bean sprouts, and soy-based seasoning.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mee Tai Bak silver needle noodles. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI ~53, low category), which is the closest analogous food — both are made from rice flour and have similar starch gelatinisation characteristics. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (53 × 21.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 39.9 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving of wok fried mee tai bak contributes approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or consume smaller portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 53 (low-medium boundary) and a glycemic load of approximately 40 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from wok oil and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate total fat (predominantly unsaturated from cooking oil) make this dish a moderate cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose dishes prepared with minimal oil, and monitor overall daily sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wok Fried Mee Tai Bak (锅炒老鼠粉) is a popular Singaporean hawker noodle dish made from mee tai bak, also known as silver needle noodles or mouse tail noodles — short, stubby, tapered rice-flour noodles named for their distinctive shape. The noodles are stir-fried at high heat in a wok with egg, bean sprouts, garlic, spring onion, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper, producing a dish with a slightly smoky wok hei aroma, a tender-chewy noodle texture, and a savoury-umami flavour profile. The dish is a staple of Chinese-Singaporean hawker culture and is commonly found at hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 518 kcal, 15.8 g protein, 16.8 g fat, and 75.3 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high at approximately 1470 mg per serving due to soy sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low-medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 40 per serving, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who should moderate portion size. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: Is wok fried mee tai bak healthy?
How many calories in mee tai bak?
Is mee tai bak suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of mee tai bak?
How is mee tai bak different from kway teow?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for wok fried mee tai bak as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo stir-fried rice noodle analogues (fried kway teow, fried bee hoon) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy cooked rice noodle data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle entry. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct match for this specific dish.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on HPB Foodo stir-fried rice noodle dishes and USDA SR Legacy rice noodle data; a direct HPB Foodo entry for mee tai bak was not identified at time of processing. Vitamin B6 is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Vitamin B12 is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Folate is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in rice-flour noodle dishes prepared with vegetable cooking oil per HPB and USDA reference data. The dish is typically prepared without pork in halal-certified hawker stalls; halal status may vary by vendor. Serving size of 350 g is a standard hawker plate estimate consistent with HPB Foodo portion guidance for stir-fried noodle dishes.
